title: Stop Saying Kids Can’t Read
gist: US reading scores have slid for 14 years, worst in middle and high school, yet Mississippi's phonics-and-accountability 'Miracle' has driven dramatic elementary gains even in its poorest county; the deeper problem is a collapse in reading stamina and willingness, blamed on smartphones, TikTok, and generative AI, that standardized-test drilling cannot fix.

insights.claim: US reading performance has declined for 14 years, with the problem most extreme in middle and high school rather than elementary grades.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Frames a durable secular decline in a core human-capital input relevant to long-run workforce quality.
insights.locator: Across the country, reading performance has slid for 14 years

insights.claim: By 2025, NAEP reading scores for 13-year-olds had plunged to 256, just one point above the 1971 level, erasing gains made in the early 21st century, with one-third of 13-year-olds scoring 'below basic'.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: A half-century of educational progress effectively wiped out is a stark macro signal on human capital.
insights.locator: Then reading performance began to drop again

insights.claim: Researchers from Harvard, Stanford, and Dartmouth declared earlier this year that the US is in 'a learning recession'.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Elite academic framing of a 'learning recession' elevates the issue to a systemic economic concern.
insights.locator: Earlier this year, a team of researchers

insights.claim: US adult literacy is deteriorating: a 2022 evaluation found 28 percent of Americans cannot reliably compare and contrast, paraphrase, or make basic inferences, up from 19 percent six years earlier, and even those with some higher education got worse.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Declining adult functional literacy has direct implications for workforce productivity and training costs.
insights.locator: A 2022 evaluation of adult literacy found

insights.claim: Mississippi rose from 49th out of 50 on NAEP fourth-grade reading in 2013 to ninth in the country by 2024, the poorest state in the US posting the most-discussed education turnaround.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that structured phonics plus accountability can move outcomes even in high-poverty settings, a replicable policy template.
insights.locator: In 2013, Mississippi ranked 49th out of 50

insights.claim: In Jefferson County, one of the poorest US counties where 98 percent of district students are Black and 32 percent live below the poverty line, fourth-grade MAAP pass rates jumped from 43 percent in 2022 to 78 percent in 2025, a 35-point gain.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Extreme local gains in a high-poverty district undercut the assumption that demographics determine reading outcomes.
insights.locator: Jefferson County’s growth curve was even steeper

insights.claim: The superintendent argues the third-grade 'reading gate'—automatic retention after failing the test three times—works not by removing weak readers from the fourth-grade pool but by making families treat passing as non-negotiable, since no Jefferson County third grader was held back in 2024 or 2025.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Identifies the behavioral incentive, not selection bias, as the driver, informing how the model might transfer elsewhere.
insights.locator: Hammitte believes the third-grade “reading gate”

insights.claim: Mississippi's Literacy-Based Promotion Act funded literacy coaches and teacher training, now $15 million a year up from $9.5 million, with a 57-coach K-3 team the associate state superintendent credits as the single biggest success factor over the reading gate or uniform materials.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Points to teacher coaching, not testing mechanics, as the underappreciated lever, redirecting where education dollars should flow.
insights.quote: it’s boots on the ground with our literacy coaches
insights.locator: Aside from its accountability measures

insights.claim: Mississippi shifted to state-approved phonics-based curricula grounded in the science of reading, rejecting the 'whole language' and 'balanced literacy' methods that had children guessing at words rather than sounding them out.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The pedagogy shift is the intellectual core of the turnaround and a signal for edtech and curriculum vendors aligned to phonics.
insights.locator: Those materials are based on scientific research

insights.claim: There is zero evidence that Mississippi's improved reading scores result from widespread fraud, and selection-bias explanations collapse because retention is not driving the gains.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Debunking the skeptic narratives strengthens the case for treating the model as genuinely replicable.
insights.locator: Most of these theories collapse under scrutiny

insights.claim: The Mississippi Miracle cracks basic literacy but success peters out at translating words into conceptual thinking: 59 percent of Jefferson County third graders were proficient in reading versus just 24 percent of eighth graders.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The proficiency cliff between grades shows decoding gains don't automatically become comprehension, the harder unsolved problem.
insights.locator: Fifty-nine percent of Jefferson County third graders

insights.claim: Teachers and literacy experts nearly unanimously diagnose the reading problem as one of attention span and stamina, not intelligence, with students quitting the moment a passage becomes uninteresting.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Reframes the crisis from cognitive capacity to engagement, implying interventions must target motivation and attention.
insights.locator: In conversations with teachers and literacy experts

insights.claim: US middle-school reading scores began declining a couple of years before elementary scores, eighth-grade NAEP performance is now one point shy of an all-time low, and the number of eighth graders scoring 'below basic' is the highest ever recorded.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The adolescent-literacy collapse is worse than the headline elementary story and less amenable to the Mississippi fix.
insights.locator: Nationwide, middle-school reading scores began their decline

themes.theme: Screens, TikTok and AI as suspects

insights.claim: A 2022 Developmental Neuropsychology paper synthesizing 11 studies found 10 identified a correlation between children's screen time and attention spans, with TikTok—used daily by 61 percent of American teens—training students to flick away from content the millisecond it becomes uninteresting.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Links short-form video consumption habits directly to eroded reading stamina, a behavioral headwind for literacy interventions.
insights.locator: The prime suspect is smartphones

insights.claim: Generative AI means students never need to struggle to understand a concept, undermining the 'productive struggle' teachers see as essential, with the author arguing students were already losing that instinct before AI's rise.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: AI removes the friction that builds comprehension skills, a second-order risk to human cognitive development beyond convenience.
insights.locator: You can’t change the reading passage

insights.claim: Many schools now introduce AI starting in kindergarten to prepare kids for an AI-normal future, but the author doubts these tools will improve true literacy since students may never develop the cognitive skills to do the work themselves.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Skepticism toward early AI adoption in schools challenges the edtech growth narrative on genuine learning outcomes.
insights.locator: As in-school apps began incorporating generative artificial intelligence

insights.claim: An education scholar says student reading engagement is gone even as students read more than ever, but only in snippets rather than books, distinguishing volume of reading from depth.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The snippet-vs-sustained-reading distinction reframes what 'literacy decline' actually measures.
insights.quote: they’re not reading books for the love of books. They’re reading probably more than they ever have, but it’s snippets.
insights.locator: “There’s no question that the engagement’s gone,”

themes.theme: Accountability testing regime and its limits

insights.claim: High-stakes standardized testing has escalated since 1983's 'A Nation at Risk' through No Child Left Behind, yet at least one original author later admitted the commission cherry-picked data to fit a preexisting thesis that schools were failing.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The foundational panic driving decades of testing policy rested on selective data, cautioning against current alarmism.
insights.locator: In the four decades since

insights.claim: Research is mixed at best on whether attaching harsher consequences to standardized tests actually improves performance, undercutting the conservative reform push for a passing requirement at every grade.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Weak evidence for high-stakes escalation argues against doubling down on the testing regime.
insights.locator: The research, though, is mixed at best

insights.claim: In test-focused states the entire year's curriculum is determined by what's on the exam, so 'reading' means skill drills rather than books, with Jefferson County using identical scripted lesson plans teachers cannot customize.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The test-driven curriculum optimizes for scores while potentially failing to build durable reading habits.
insights.locator: The reading gate is part of a broader array

insights.claim: The author concludes that if America pulls out of the test-score slump it will happen through skill drills, not students losing themselves in novels, because human flourishing is not as easily measurable as student achievement.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Captures the core tension: measurable test gains may crowd out unmeasurable genuine literacy.
insights.quote: Human flourishing is not as easily measurable as student achievement
insights.locator: America’s fervor for standardized tests

insights.claim: Only 16 percent of Americans read for fun per a 2023 study, down from 28 percent in 2004, and only 41 percent of children under 4 are frequently read to, down 23 points since 2012.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: Collapsing early read-aloud and adult pleasure-reading signals the pipeline of engaged readers is drying up structurally.
insights.locator: Just 16 percent of Americans read for fun

insights.claim: Literacy scholars say the ideal method is assigning students texts on subjects they choose so they build stamina, but test-driven curricula leave no time for whole books and ignore student interest.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: The gap between evidence-based practice and test-driven reality highlights where curriculum reform could add value.
insights.locator: Literacy scholars agree that teaching reading across subjects

insights.claim: For the first time in her career an eighth-grade teacher cannot name a single student across five class periods who reads for fun, even among her strongest readers.
insights.speaker: Megan Greenwell
insights.so_what: A frontline signal that willingness to read, not just ability, has collapsed even among capable students.
insights.quote: I don’t have any of them that has a book.
insights.locator: This year, for the first time in her career
